I. Introduction

Discussions relating to a new economic and financial program that would succeed Zambia's rights accumulation program (RAP) were held in Lusaka in February, April, and May l995. l/ The staff met with the Minister of Finance, Mr. R.D.S.. Penza; the outgoing Governor of the Bank of Zambia, Mr. D. Mulaisho; the new Governor, Dr. J. Mwanza; and other senior officials, as well as representatives of the private sector.

Zambia's RAP, endorsed by the Executive Board on July 17, 1992, provided for the accumulation of rights by Zambia for up to SDR 836.9 million (EBS/92/ll4, 7/l/92) during the period to end-March 1995. In completing the latest review of the RAP on September 23, 1994 (EBM/94/90), the Executive Board granted waivers for the nonobservance of performance criteria relating to net claims on Government, external payments arrears, and civil service wages, thus permitting the accumulation of additional rights in an amount equaling SDR 92.98 million, for a total of SDR 647.00 million (Table l).

Macroeconomic performance through the end of l994 was broadly satisfactory, although nonobservance of certain performance criteria prevented the final accumulations of rights, scheduled for November 15, 1994 and February 15, l995. In the early part of 1995, however, serious difficulties in fiscal control developed, exacerbated by the provision of substantial financial support to a troubled commercial bank. These difficulties delayed the development of a successor program and prevented the granting of waivers for the nonobservance of performance criteria. In May l995, the authorities adopted and began to implement an economic and financial program to correct the policy slippages.

On June l4, l995, the Executive Board reviewed again the matter of Zambia's overdue financial obligations to the Fund (EBS/95/8l, 5/l8/95). Al that meeting, Executive Directors commended the Zambian authorities for their-achievements in restoring.macroeconomic stability and restructuring the economy during the period of the RAP. They also commented favorably on Zambia's strong payments record to the Fund since l992. In view of the policy slippages that had occurred in the first half of l995, however, a majority of Executive Directors felt that time was needed to assess the implementation and effects of the proposed policy measures. The Executive Board decided to consider the completion of the RAP and a request for successor arrangements after there was evidence of satisfactory economic performance up to the end of September l995. The period of the rights accumulation program has been extended, and a request for a further extension to December 15, 1995 is expected shortly.

Since June, the Zambian authorities have proceeded with implementation of the new program. Performance through September 1995 was in general consistent with program objectives (see EBD/95/122, 9/6/95, and Supplement 1, 10/l9/95). A staff visit to Lusaka in October 1995 confirmed that, with steady implementation, the program objectives for the remainder of 1995 could be achieved.

In the attached letter dated November 2l, 1995, and the accompanying memorandum on economic and financial policies (MEFP) (Appendix I), the Minister of Finance describes the new economic program that Zambia is pursuing during the period 1995/96-1997/98. 1/ He also requests approval of the waivers necessary for the final accumulation of rights under the RAP. Once Zambia has settled its overdue financial obligations to the Fund and consented to and paid for its quota increase under the Ninth General Review of Quotas, a request by Zambia for use of Fund resources under parallel structural adjustment facility (SAF) and enhanced structural adjustment facility (ESAF) arrangements will be submitted for the consideration of the Executive Board. 2/ Access under the SAF would be in an amount equivalent to SDR 18l.7 million (50 percent of Zambia's new quota of SDR 363.5 million under the Ninth General Review), and access under the ESAF would be SDR 70l.7 million (l93 percent of new quota). In the first year, a total of SDR 843.4 million (232 percent of new quota) would be available, of which SDR 830.2 million would represent the encashment of rights accumulated under the RAP. 3/ Zambia's program is to be implemented in the context of a medium-term strategy, which has been formulated by the authorities in close collaboration with the staffs of the Fund and the World Bank and is set out in a policy framework paper (PFP) (forthcoming) for the period 1995-98.

Zambia was declared ineligible to use the Fund's general resources oni September 30, l987. When the decision on the accumulation of rights was approved on July 17, 1992, Zambia's arrears to the Fund stood at SDR 920.7 million. Zambia has made payments to the Fund in excess of newly maturing obligations under the RAP, and on November 1, l995, the arrears amounted to SDR 830.2 million. Zambia's outstanding use of Fund credit at end-October 1995 was SDR 548.8 million, or l5l percent of the new quota (Appendix II). Zambia's position with the Fund during the period of the envisaged parallel SAF and ESAF arrangements is shown in Table 2.

Zambia eliminated arrears to the World Bank on January 3l, l992, and has met its obligations to the Bank since that time. The Bank has approved a series of structural adjustment loans since that time, most recently an Economic Recovery and Investment Promotion Credit (ERIP), totaling SDR 90 million, on July ll, 1995. The Bank is also financing various sectoral projects (Appendix III).

Aspects of performance under the RAP are presented in Appendix IV. Basic economic and financial data and social and demographic indicators are presented in Appendices V and VI, respectively. Medium-term prospects and debt sustainability will be discussed in a forthcoming supplement.
II. Performance Under the Rights Accumulation Program, 1992-95
1. Background

In November l99l, when the current Government of Zambia took office, the Zambian economy had long been in decline, with per capita income down by one third since the early l970s. The decline reflected a sharp and prolonged fall in the real price of copper, Zambia's dominant export, as well as a substantial decline in Zambia's copper production, exacerbated by efficiency problems. But economic policy also played a role: pervasive economic controls and domination of the economy by parastatal enterprises inhibited private initiative and impeded economic growth. During the 1970s and 1980s, attempts were made to offset declining incomes through heavy external borrowing, leading to major debt service problems and accumulation of arrears to virtually all multilateral, bilateral, and commercial creditors, and .resulting in a cessation of external financial support. In these circumstances, it was impossible to maintain the improvements that had been made in the .l960s and early 1970s in health and education, and by the 1990s social services had also deteriorated.

. The new Government's Economic Recovery Program was designed to reverse the economic decline through far-reaching market-oriented reforms, more efficient use of resources by the public sector, cessation of inflationary financing, and restoration of normal relations with the international financial community. The program was supported by the RAP, a series of World Bank structural adjustment loans, and new financing by bilateral donors, as well as debt rescheduling and reduction.
2. Policy implementation and results
a. Overview

As' summarized in Appendix IV, Zambia has made great strides in some areas of structural reform, and more limited progress in others; a few areas of reform are still at an early stage, however. In general, the most rapid progress has been made with structural reforms that involved the freeing of markets and elimination of government intervention and control; indeed, in many respects the liberalization has gone much further than was envisaged at the outset of the RAP. l/ Thus, the omnipresent controls on external, financial, and agricultural activities and transactions have all given way to market mechanisms. Parastatal companies have had their subsidies removed and have been required to operate on a market-oriented basis; a number of those that have not been able to do so successfully have been liquidated.

By contrast, progress has been slow where institution building or the development of new mechanisms for institutional reform was required. After a slow start, the privatization program is now proceeding well, but the most important privatization, that of ZCCM, the mining parastatal, is at a very early stage. Employment in parastatal enterprises is being rationalized through subjection to market forces and privatization, but reform of the civil service, characterized by excessive employment and very low salaries, is just beginning. There has been a significant upgrading of the staff and capacity for monetary policy implementation of the Bank of Zambia (BoZ), but the BoZ's capacity for bank supervision still needs substantial strengthening.

Over the course of the RAP, inflationary fiscal and quasi-fiscal operations have frequently hindered effective policy implementation (see Appendix IV). In each of the years l992-95, new fiscal or quasi-fiscal problems emerged in the first half of the year, leading to a resurgence of inflation, a sharp depreciation of the kwacha, and rising interest rates, and in each year corrective actions were taken at midyear, resulting in renewed progress on stabilization in the second half. Although the policy response in each year included ad hoc revenue measures and expenditure cuts, fundamental reforms were also undertaken, as summarized in Appendix IV.
b． Program implementation since mid-l994 and request for waivers

The macroeconomic performance criteria on net domestic assets of the BoZ, net claims on Government, and net international reserves were met with some room to spare in September and December 1994, and most other performance criteria and benchmarks were observed (Table 3). However, the performance criteria relating to government wage increases, external arrears, and Zambia Airways were not observed. In the letter attached as Appendix I, the Minister of Finance requests that the nonobservance of these three performance criteria be waived, thus permitting Zambia to accumulate the remaining rights under the RAP.

The facts regarding the nonobservance of the three performance criteria are set out in paragraphs 6-8 of the MEFP. The breach of the criterion relating to government wages was a technical one and stemmed from a projection error. The large arrears on programmed external debt service payments at September 30, l994 reflected continuing shortcomings in debt service procedures, but there was a further strengthening of procedures after that.

Arrears declined to ${\tt U S S1.2}$ million by December 3l, 1994, and were eliminated shortly after that date. l/ In l995, Zambia has remained current on its programmed external debt service payments, 2/ which exclude obligations for which Paris Club rescheduling is being sought. 3/ A decision on the future of Zambia Airways, which had been expected by October 31, l994, was delayed, and before the decision was taken on December 2, 1994, some additional financial support was provided by the Government. The applicable performance criterion was breached, but the eventual decision to close the airline was an important reaffirmation of the Government's policy of requiring parastatal companies to operate on a commercial basis or be closed. A subsequent example was the closure of the United Bus Company of Zambia.

With steady program implementation in the second half of 1994, inflation was reduced to an annual rate of 22 percent, compared with 50 percent in both the first half of 1994 and the last half of l993. Interest rates declined rapidly, to a low of 19 percent in November, and the exchange rate was virtually stable (Chart l). Toward the end of l994, however, inflation began to pick up because of rising maize meal prices. Another drought loomed and confidence appeared to be faltering: the kwacha began to depreciate and interest rates to rise. When serious policy problems began to emerge in February, the response in the markets was severe. In the first half of l995, prices rose at an annual rate of 32 percent, and the kwacha depreciated by 35 percent, despite large net sales of foreign exchange by the BoZ that virtually depleted the available international reserves. Interest rates rose again to exceed 60 percent by midyear.

A principal source of the problem was the fiscal and quasi-fiscal support provided on a large scale, beginning in February, to Meridien BIAO Zambia, one of Zambia's largest commercial banks. By May, when the bank was closed, total public sector support had reached K 5l billion (over $13$ percent of GDP), and $\mathtt{U S}\$25$ million of BoZ reserves lodged with Meridien's Bahamas-based parent, which was also in receivership, were no longer available.

At the same time, independently of the problems with Meridien, fiscal control was slipping. In the first few months of l995, revenues underperformed and expenditures, particularly on defense, rose above budgeted levels, raising the possibility of a fiscal gap in l995 as large as 3 percent of GDP. Remedial fiscal action was clearly necessary and the measures taken constitute the core of the first year of the new program. l/
c. Outcome

Since economic reforms begar at the end of l99l, Zambia has been buffeted by mild or severe drougtt (in three years out of the four), and by falling copper production, although the latter has been mitigated since mid-1994 by unexpectedly high copper prices. Combined with the recurrent episodes of macroeconomic instability, these difficulties have meant that the improvements in economic incentives have yet to result in economic growth; in fact, real GDP in l995 is likely to be lower than it was in 199l. Nonetheless, positive effects are beginning to emerge.
The inflation rate has declined sharply, from triple-digit rates for several years up to l992 to an average annual rate of 38 percent since mid-1993.

The allocation of financial resources has been improved through the maintenance of positive real interest rates and through the sharp reduction in the spread between lending and deposit rates through reductions in reserve requirements.
Nontraditional exports have risen by 20 percent annually over the last three years.
The planting of maize has declined in favor of more droughtresistant staples and commercial crops.

While high real interest rates and the instability of the kwacha have inhibited strong growth in new investment, the elimination of exchange controls means that development of foreign exchange-based activity, such as tourism, has accelerated.

1/ Key revenue measures included the introduction of a temporary 5 percentage point import declaration fee and a temporary exemption of ZCCM from the value-added tax (VAT), which reduced refunds by some 2 percent cf GDP (annual rate). Additional details on the nature and extent of the stabilization problem that developed in the first half of 1995 are to be found in paragraphs l0-l6 of the MEFP and in the note entitled "Zambia - Information Meeting for the Executive Board - Speaking Notes for the Staff Representative," which was circulated by the Acting Secretary on June 9. 1995.
Substantial privatization receipts in l995 mainly reflect direct foreign investment, and privatized companies are undertaking additional investment.
The domestic fiscal deficit was about 7 percent of GDP in 1991. In l995, the domestic budget has been nearly in balance, and domestic arrears from $1994$ are being cleared as well.
The strengthening of the fiscal position has been accomplished by a reduction in domestic expenditures from 25 percent of GDP in l99l to a projected level in l995 of less than 18 percent.

The main cuts were in subsidies and agricultural expenditure, but other current and capital expenditures also decreased. The social sectors were adversely affected, but government wages and defense expenditures also declined relative to GDP.

The ratio of revenue to GDP recovered in l994 and l995 from a sharp fall in l993; it is now back to the l99l level of about 19 percent, despite lower mining revenues. Establishment of an independent revenue authority and the introduction of a VAT are strengthening revenue performance.
III. The Program for 1995/96-1997/98 1/
1. Medium-term policy framework
a. Overall strategy

The economic program for 1995/96-1997/98 aims at strengthening the macroeconomic stabilization effort, while consolidating and advancing the structural reforms initiated under the RAP. The objectives of the program include a single-digit inflation rate; a surplus of l percent of GDP on the Government's domestic budget; 2/ a significant accumulation of foreign exchange reserves; and completion of the process of liberalizing the economy (paragraphs l8-27 of the MEFP). 3/

In achieving these goals, the program aims to secure the foundations for sustained economic growth. Investment is projected to increase by 10 percentage points of GDP between 1994 and l998, and real GDP is projected to grow by 6 percent annually in l996-98 (Table 4), as agriculture recovers from drought and copper output reverses its recent sharp decline. Economic growth of this magnitude will help to reverse the decline in average living standards witnessed in Zambia over the last two decades.

The mix of policies is also designed to begin the necessary strengthening of the external sector by encouraging the growth of nontraditional exports; however, given the weak prospects for copper, this process will stretch out for many years beyond the program period. Thus, to finance the higher level of investment, and until the point when new copper mines start to produce and nontraditional exports have grown substantially, Zambia will need to increase its use of foreign saving. Nonetheless, with the Government reducing its current expenditure and the private sector enjoying enhanced profitability, domestic saving will rise by over 4 percentage points of GDP over the program period.

Sustained economic growth requires not only higher investment, but also an increase in Zambia's ability to withstand external shocks. The BoZ's medium-term goal is to build gross foreign reserves to a level equivalent to four months of nonmaize imports by 200o, with most of the necessary increase taking place during the program period. Besides boosting confidence in the macroeconomic management of the economy, an accumulation of reserves on this scale is considered prudent in light of continuing vulnerability to drought, the perceived temporary nature of currently high copper prices, and the heavy debt service burden projected for the medium term. 1/

The authorities' fiscal objectives are to support the stabilization effort and to pare down expenditures to a sustainable level, while freeing financial resources for the development of the private sector. The reduction in domestic spending under the RAP to about 18 percent of GDP will be sustained during the program. Meanwhile, revenue will be maintained at about l9 percent of GDP, implying a switch in the domestic budget balance from a deficit of $23$ percent of GDP in l994 to a surplus of 1 percent of GDP per year from 1996 onward (Table 5). 2/

The far-reaching structural reforms achieved under the RAP will be reinforced and extended during the new economic program. One priority will be reform of the financial sector and a strengthening of the banking system. (Aside from its critical role in Zambia's development, such a strengthening is needed to reduce the risk of quasi-fiscal expenditures through financial support for troubled banks.) Other priorities include the privatization program for the parastatal sector (particularly for the copper company, ZCcM), the reform of the civil service, and the completion of the Government's withdrawal from agricultural financing and marketing.
b. Fiscal policy

Tax reform is prominent on the Zambian fiscal agenda. The guiding principles are that there should be less reliance on trade taxation; l/ that the overall package of tax changes should be revenue neutral; and that any increases in tax revenue relative to GDP should come from improving administration and compliance. The newly introduced VAT should allow trade taxes to be reduced over the program period to a maximum of 25 percent, while the recently established Zambian Revenue Authority (ZRA), which has a large degree of independence and the resources to hire high-quality staff, can be expected to further enhance tax collections.

The domestic fiscal surpluses targeted over the program period will reduce the Government's domestic debt and, in conjunction with the expected decline in interest rates, lead to sharp reductions in domestic interest expenditure. Even within a declining total budget, this will permit increased allocations to social and capital expenditures. 2/ Continuing efforts are being made to strengthen expenditure control, including a commitments monitoring system, with particular emphasis on controlling defense spending (paragraphs 37 and 38).

A critical element in fiscal discipline over the program period will be the size of the annual wage increase granted to civil servants. In formulating budgets during the program, wage awards are to be set so as to prevent the real wage bill from increasing. This policy will imply that personal emoluments fall as a percentage of GDP over the program period, but, provided that the Public Sector Reform Program (PSRP) succeeds in reducing the size of the civil service, will permit wage increases in excess of inflation to qualified staff.

The privatization program promises to generate significant receipts for the budget, although it is first necessary to provide for any costs of retrenching parastatal employees that fall on the privatization agency. The authorities intend to use net privatization receipts to reduce government borrowing, and, in due course, for one-time expenditures that make no recurrent claim on budget resources and that will be consistent with the overall fiscal program (paragraph 45).

In its conduct of monetary policy over the program period, the BoZ will endeavor to keep inflat:ion on a downward path. The exchange rate will continue to float; the BoZ's interventions in the foreign exchange market will be limited to making the purchases necessary to meet the programmed buildup in reserves, and to smoothing short-term fluctuations. l/ The process of building reserves, in conjunction with a credit policy geared toward low inflation, will help to ensure that the exchange rate provides signals appropriate to promoting external viability.

The demand for real money balances in Zambia can be expected to increase over time, particularly once inflation resumes its downward trend and economic growth begins. 2/ Until there is clear evidence of increased money demand, however, the authorities will be careful not to assume such an increase in framing their monetary policy objectives. With the Government making repayments to the banking system, such a policy nonetheless would allow some real growth in bank credit to the private sector. However, should inflation threaten to reignite, or should the exchange rate come under sustained downward pressure, credit policy will be tightened appropriately.

Under the RAP, the BoZ moved to using open market operations in its conduct of monetary policy and to making less use of direct monetary instruments such as the statutory reserve ratio. The indirect instrument approach will become fully functional early in the new program with the recapitalization of the BoZ. This will endow the monetary authorities with a portfolio of performing assets suitable for use in open market operations.

During l995, the BoZ was on occasion constrained in its ability to tighten monetary conditions by the need to provide liquidity to specific banks in distress and by concerns about the vulnerability of other banks. Over the medium term, strict enforcement of new banking regulations, some of which have already been issued, as well as enhanced banking supervision, will do much to strengthen the domestic banking system, thereby preventing a recurrence of the problems experienced in l995. l/ Moreover, to limit costly quasi-fiscal support for banks that run into difficulty, the authorities intend to take steps to identify banking problems at an early stage and to take decisive action when necessary (paragraph 61).

The authorities intend to meet their external debt obligations in a timely manner and to avoid incurring any external arrears during the program period. Medium- and long-term borrowing on a nonconcessional basis will be avoided. No new external debt swaps will be entered into outside the ambit of official debt reduction operations. Over time, the BoZ will be retiring its short-term commercial debt, a process that is currently being affected by litigations by some commercial creditors, as discussed below. Once the BoZ's short-term debt is settled, the authorities will have taken important steps toward rehabilitating Zambia's international creditworthiness. They will also have eliminated the last remaining restriction on current exchange transactions, facilitating Zambia's acceptance of the obligations of Article VIII of the Fund's Articles of Agreements.
d. Structural policies

During the RAP, the authorities successfully dismantled the panoply of controls and regulations that characterized the economy at the beginning of the l990s. However, state-owned firms continue to control major sectors of the economy, and there is a pressing need to improve the efficiency of the civil service. The privatization program and the PSRP will therefore be core areas of structural reform in the period ahead.

Under the privatization program, Government is divesting itself of extensive holdings in companies throughout the economy. A major acceleration of the program has taken place this year and it is expected to be largely completed by 1998. 2/ The most important step will be putting the Government's majority shareholding in ZCCM up for sale (paragraphs 64-67).

The Zambian civil service, while not large by regional standards, employs more people than are needed at salaries that militate against retaining skills. With the aim of attaining a smaller, better-paid, and more efficient civil service, the authorities have embarked on the PSRP; during l995 the restructuring of several ministries is beginning.

The Government will complete the process of withdrawing from intervention in the agricultural sector, apart from maintenance of a strategic maize reserve (paragraph 27). As described in the previous section, priority is also being given to structural reforms in the financial sector.
e. Social policies

Poverty is pervasive in Zambia. World Bank estimates for l99l suggest that at that time as many as 55 percent of Zambians lacked the means to satisfy basic nutritional requirements, with an even higher incidence of absolute poverty in rural areas. The best hope for a general increase in living standards and a sustainable reduction in poverty in Zambia is the resumption of economic growth expected to result from sound structural adjustment policies. The authorities are also seeking to alleviate poverty by reorienting public expenditure toward the social sectors, and by providing training and limited financial support to workers laid off in the privatization process. In addition, drought-relief programs are currently being run with donor-provided maize.
2. Program for 1995/96 1/
a. Macroeconomic framework

The drought is now estimated to have reduced the l994/95 maize crop to 738,000 tons, some 30 percent lower than the l993/94 harvest, which itself had been affected by drought. With mining also declining, real GDP is not expected to record positive growth in l995 despite some recovery in the rest of the economy.

The high costs of importing maize into Zambia (including transport) imply that domestic maize meal prices will rise dramatically in late 1995 and early 1996, when local supplies become exhausted. Moreover, the jump in prices will be larger if, as seems likely, it proves necessary to import from more expensive sources outside the region. 2/ Increases in maize meal prices have already begun to occur and the further rises now anticipated by the end of the year make the original end-l995 inflation target of 25 percent no longer attainable: the actual figure will be at least
35 percent. However, by mid-l996, maize meal prices should have returned to more normal levels, and the original end-1996 inflation target of 10 percent has been retained.

In light of the lapses in financial control in the first half of the year,fixed investment has probably not increased in l995 as had been hoped for in light of the structural reforms and macroeconomic progress in 1994. Nonetheless, with business confidence recovering, fixed investment is projected to expand by $23$ percentage points of GDP in l996, financed by additional domestic saving.
b. Fiscal policy

The measures taken in mid-l995 l/ were designed to generate a surplus in the domestic budget in l995 of about 1 percent of GDP. This would be sufficient to permit the clearance of domestic arrears from $1994$ , and hence to achieve the original l995 budget objective of no recourse to domestic borrowing for the year as a whole. The l996 budget, to be presented in January, will again target a domestic surplus of 1 percent of GDP (with no new arrears to be paid off), implying a surplus on the domestic budget over the program year l995/96 (July-June) of about the same magnitude.

The l996 budget's provision for a wage award will be designed to prevent any real increase in the wage bill. 2/ In their discussions with the civil service unions, the authorities will stress that wage claims in 1996 in excess of the inflation rate underlying the budget can only be paid for by additional revenue measures, thus subjecting any attempt to increase real wages to scrutiny by the Zambian public.

With regard to tax reform in l996, the authorities intend to broaden the income tax base to include noncash fringe benefits, while using the revenue so generated to reduce income tax rates. Initially, this reform is likely to result in slightly lower pay-as-you-earn (PAYE) receipts, but over the medium term will improve the fairness and buoyancy of the tax system. A rationalization of the customs duty structure is planned for the medium term; in l996, there will be particular emphasis on the scope for limiting or rolling back duty exemptions. The authorities also plan to remove the duty exemption on government imports, which, by closing an avenue for tax evasion, should have a net beneficial effect on the budget.

In 1995, any net privatization receipts in excess of K l4 billion will be used to reduce borrowing from the banking system. Given the continuing need to reinforce financial stability, it is likely that most such receipts will need to be used for the same purpose in 1996.

In the period since the l995/96 program was outlined to the Executive Board, the authorities have proceeded with its implementation. Key fiscal policy actions have been taken, albeit with delay or modification in some cases. l/ Because of Zambia's difficulties in meeting donor conditionality, there has been a significant shortfall in balance of payments assistance in 1995, which has necessitated government borrowing from the BoZ in order to meet external debt service. This borrowing peaked at $\mathtt{U S S51}$ million in August, and has since begun to unwind. In order to remain within the end-September indicative benchmark on net claims on Government (NCG) in spite of this borrowing, the authorities maintained tight control on expenditures, overperforming on the domestic budget and postponing much of the scheduled clearance of domestic arrears. Except for the delay in clearing domestic arrears, fiscal performance has been consistent with program targets. 2/

During the recent staff visit to Lusaka, the staff noted that meeting the NCG performance criterion for end-December, with full clearance of domestic arrears, might require continued expenditure restraint in the fourth quarter in view of a possible revenue shortfall and higher-thanprogrammed expenditures in some categories. 3/ The staff discussed with the authorities contingency plans for keeping expenditures on recurrent departmental charges (RDCs) and capital projects within an overall envelope for l995 as much as K 22 billion below what had been programmed last May, which would still permit fourth quarter noninterest expenditures higher than the drastically compressed levels earlier in the year (Table 6). Operation of the cash budget would itself imply the necessary degree of expenditure restraint, but the authorities indicated that they intend to administer any necessary cuts in a judicious and predictable fashion. Thus, ministries would be advised in advance of the minimum allocations they could count on. Currently active capital projects would be fully funded, but new or inactive projects would be left on hold until the availability of resources was assured. Key social spending would be maintained, but defense and general administration would not be fully funded; as much as possible, cuts would be focussed on nonessential areas such as domestic and international travel.

The 1995/96 program anticipates 12-month broad money growth of 32 percent through December 1995 (Table 7), $\pmb{\underline{{1}}}/$ while net international reserves (NIR) are to be built up by at least $\mathtt{U S S61}$ million in the first six months of the program (Table 8). The end-December floor for NIR has been set so as to recoup most of the reserve losses of the first half of l995, even before taking account of the expected yield of some ${\tt U S S60}$ million from the contingency mechanism for excess copper earnings and donor flows. Targets for the first six months of 1996 are provisional at this stage, but it is envisaged that money growth would be about 13 percent (annualized) with a further minimum foreign reserve accumulation of $\mathtt{U S}\$26$ million; on the conservative assumption of no further accumulation under the contingency mechanism after end-l995, gross reserves at end-June l996 would be equivalent to $23$ months of imports of goods and nonfactor services.

The need for financial system reform has been further demonstrated by the failure of African Commercial Bank, a small bank that was put into receivership on November l3. 2/ Two key banking regulations, on capital adequacy and the treatment of fixed assets, went into effect on November 17, and further regulations are to be gazetted in the first year of the program (paragraph 57). In addition, the BoZ will be recapitalized with performing assets to facilitate the conduct of monetary policy. 3/ It will be permitted to pay and charge interest to the Government, and charge fees to the commercial banks for supervision services. During l996, the BoZ will strengthen its supervisory capacity and improve the quality of both on- and off-site inspections. It will also develop a system of prudential indicators for early warning of banking problems, and will establish objective criteria for determining bank solvency.

Through September, the indicative benchmarks for NIR and the net domestic assets of the BoZ (NDA) were observed. The first quarter of the program year also saw ZCcM becoming current in its obligations under the copper contingency mechanism: by end-September, the company had sold the BoZ about $\mathtt{U S S35}$ million in excess copper earnings in exchange for treasury bills. Through October, the level of net international reserves remained above the end-December target.

Monetary growth has been high in the program period to date; initially this reflected problems in reining in NDA, l/ but since August they have been brought under control; the continued monetary growth reflects the rapid increase in international reserves and an increase in the money multiplier. This may reflect growing confidence in the banking system, as the share of bank deposits in the total money stock is rising. Nonetheless, to guard against any rekindling of inflationary pressures, the staff has recommended restraining the growth of reserve money by sterilizing most of any continuing excess in NIR over the program floor. 2/ Such restraint could imply some reduction in credit to the private sector, but the impact would be mitigated by the programmed reduction in government arrears.

The successful implementation of the l995/96 program to date has brought other signs of improved confidence. Initially, the kwacha continued to depreciate and interest rates on treasury bills continued to rise, but from August, the situation began to improve. Despite the BoZ's building up reserves by almost US\$ 90 million in August-October, the kwacha has traded in a narrow range against the U.S. dollar. Moreover, even though reserve money contracted in September and October, treasury bill rates declined to about 45 percent at the end of October, from their peak of over 60 percent in mid-July.

The program period has also seen legal difficulties with commercial creditors, which may eventually require foreign exchange outlays that were not taken into account in the design of the program. 3/ Furthermore, the country stands to lose foreign reserves amounting to $\mathtt{U S S37}$ million that were lodged with Meridien International and BcCI, which now face liquidation. The potential for such losses will be an important ingredient in deciding at the first review of the program on the future pace of reserve accumulation and the policies needed to achieve it.

The authorities intend to seek a Paris Club flow rescheduling on Naples terms through the end of the three-year program period. Since the expiry of the 1992 Paris Club agreement on March 31, 1995, and pending a new rescheduling agreement after approval of a new Fund arrangement, Zambia has continued to make payments on post-cutoff debt and moratorium interest from the 1992 rescheduling as if the l992 agreement were still in effect.
d. Structural policies

The process of determining the staffing requirements of ministries under the PSRP's restructuring should be largely completed by the end of 1995. This exercise aims at identifying the areas of Government with excessive employment so as to provide a basis for retrenchment decisions. However, the existing severance arrangements for civil servants are such as to make the cost of retrenchment prohibitive and, at least in the short term, it appears to be politically difficult to revise the relevant legislation. In the interim, the authorities intend to reduce employment through attrition and are currently implementing a partial hiring freeze, under which only one hire is permitted for every three separations. It is estimated that this mechanism will reduce the size of the civil service by about 2 percent in 1995/96.

Structural reforms in the financial sector in the first year of the program were described in the previous section. Structural reforms in other areas are set out in paragraphs 47 and 64-67 of the Memorandum. Statistical issues are discussed in paragraph 68 and in more detail in the PFP.
e. Performance criteria

The program supported by the first annual ESAF arrangement will be monitored on the basis of the following quantitative performance criteria and benchmarks: (a) net domestic assets of the BoZ; (b) net claims on Government; (c) domestic arrears of the Government; (d) budgetary expenditure for debt swaps; (e) net international reserves of the BoZ; (f) the stock of external short-term debt; (g) external payments arrears; (h) new nonconcessional external medium- or long-term loans; and (i) size of the civil service (Table 8).

Other structural performance criteria and benchmarks relate to: (a) the retail pricing of petroleum products; l/ (b) gazetting of banking regulations; (c) reform of the civil service; and (d) recapitalization of the Boz. Performance criteria will apply for end-December l995 under the first annual arrangement. In addition, there will be a midterm review under the first annual ESAF arrangement, to be completed no later than April 30, 1996, which will include, in particular, review of the financing of any real increase in the wage bill, the privatization of ZCcM and progress toward recovering official support for Meridien.
IV. Balance of Payments and External Financing
1. Outlook and financing requirements for l995-98

A balance of payments scenario for the program period is set out in Tables 9 and l0, and the principal elements of a scenario for the real economy and the fiscal sector appear in Table 4. The scenario assumes continuing strong policy implementation. The prospects for copper continue to dominate Zambia's current account, as metals still account for 85 percent of total exports despite the rapid growth of nontraditional exports in recent years. Copper prices are currently relatively high, but are expected to decline over the next few years, so that even if output recovers from its current decline as technical problems are overcome, metal revenues in 1998 would be below the level projected for l995. Thus, even with strong growth of nontraditional exports and nonmetal imports growing on average more slowly than GDP, the current account would worsen substantially by 1998, though in the process the foundations for a future strengthening would be laid. With the return of more favorable weather and continuing diversification toward more drought-resistant food crops, maize imports would decline next year and drop to their normal zero level by 1997.

The other element dominating Zambia's balance of payments prospects is external debt service (Table ll). Scheduled interest payments on official. debt will decrease sharply next year with the shift to concessional interest rates on Zambia's liabilities to the Fund, but would not decline further. Scheduled amortization of official debt will drop over the period, however, largely on account of Paris Club debt. Balance of payments assistance is assumed to decline considerably from the extraordinary levels of recent years, although the decline may be slowed by the need to help fill financing gaps. Project assistance is projected to gradually increase and will be an important source of financing for government capital expenditure.

A key element in Zambia's program is a substantial buildup in international reserves, both to put Zambia in a better position to deal with exogenous shocks, such as drought or changes in copper prices, and to help ensure that the exchange rate provides signals appropriate to promoting

external viability. l/ Gross reserves are projected to rise by 1998 to close to the medium-term objective of 4 months' imports of goods and nonfactor services (excluding maize) from the current level of about $23\leq$ months. The timing of the buildup will need to be determined year by year in light of the strength of the balance of payments.

The resulting financing gap is $\mathtt{U S S169}$ million in 1995 (Table 10), rising to $\mathtt{U S S220}$ million in 1996 and about $\tt U S\$200$ million in 1997 and 1998. As noted earlier, since the expiry of the l992 Paris Club agreement on March 3l, 1995, Zambia has continued to service Paris Club post-cutoff date debt and debt arising out of the l992 rescheduling; the financing gap for 1995 is equal to the amount of l995 debt service payments (on pre-cutoff date debt and previously rescheduled debt) that Zambia will be asking the Paris Club to reschedule. The gaps for l996-98 could be filled by continuing comprehensive Paris Club reschedulings, together with some additional balance of payments support. Adequate financing for the first year of the program (July l995-June l996) is assured provided the Paris Club affords the rescheduling requested and already committed balance of payments support is disbursed on a timely basis.
2. Capacity to repay the Fund

The longer-term outlook for the balance of payments and achievement of external viability will be analyzed in the forthcoming supplement on debt sustainability. A fundamental problem that is clear from that analysis is that when large SAF/ESAF repayments begin in 20ol, new copper mines will not yet have been developed and the projected growth of non-traditional exports is likely to have been largely offset by declines in the price of copper. Repayments to the Fund are thus likely to exceed 15 percent of exports of goods and nonfactor services, and the overall debt service ratio to exceed 40 percent. Zambia will clearly need further bilateral and multilateral support and comprehensive reschedulings of bilateral debt, all of which would need to be on highly concessional terms. By the time the ESAF repayments end, however, new copper mines and continued strengthening of the non-copper current account should begin to bring debt service ratios down to more manageable levels.

Zambia's capacity to repay its SAF/ESAF loans will thus be highly dependent on both sustained adjustment efforts and continued financing from creditors and donors. Zambia's record on payments to the Fund was very poor up to l992, as repeated lapses in adjustment efforts led to growing arrears. However, under the RAP, Zambia has both kept current in its debt service obligations to the Fund and made cash payments of SDR 84 million to reduce outstanding arrears. More generally, over the period of the RAP, Zambia's record of meeting programmed external debt service payments strengthened considerably, and its performance in l995 has been satisfactory.
V. Staff Appraisal

The period of the RAP has seen profound changes in the policy orientation of the Zambian authorities and a series of far-reaching structural reforms. The level of government expenditure has dropped sharply and a cash budget mechanism has been employed with the aim of avoiding inflationary financing. The domestic budget deficit has been reduced to zero, from a figure of 7 percent of GDP in 199l, thereby freeing considerable resources for the use of the private sector. Controls on interest rates have been eliminated and instruments of indirect monetary policy have been introduced; interest rates have been market determined in a weekly treasury bill auction since early l993. State intervention in the agricultural sector has been progressively curtailed: pricing and marketing have been decontrolled, and the budget subsidies that caused great difficulties in the past have been eliminated.

In the external area, foreign exchange controls have been abolished, exchange markets have been unified, and the exchange rate is now allowed to find its own level. Import licenses are now granted automatically, being retained only for statistical purposes. Zambia has strengthened its record of meeting programmed external debt service obligations over time, and has adhered to prudent policies regarding the incurrence of new external debt. Arrears on commercial debt have been largely eliminated through a buy-back.

In the second half of $1994$ , when the latest review of the RAP was concluded, Zambia strengthened its policy performance in a number of areas, and most of the program performance criteria were observed in September and December l994. The authorities have asked for waivers for the nonobservance of three performance criteria for December $1994$ to permit the accumulation of the rights remaining under the RAP. As described in the authorities' policy memorandum and in the text of this report, the nonobservance of the performance criterion on government wages was technical, the nonobservance of the performance criterion on external arrears represented problems in policy implementation that have since been corrected, and the nonobservance of the performance criterion relating to Zambia Airways was also corrected by subsequent policy action. In the view of the staff, it would be appropriate that the nonobservance of these performance criteria be waived.

The setback to macroeconomic stability of the events of early 1995 now seems to have been largely resolved. The authorities have been resolute in their implementation of the new program, despite a large shortfall in donor assistance. There are encouraging signs that confidence is returning and continued adherence to the program holds out the promise of output and employment growth in 1996.

The authorities are well aware of the suboptimality of repeatedly having to devise revenue measures in response to fiscal gaps which emerge during the course of the fiscal year. Such revenue measures typically involve ad hoc tax increases, thus raising average tax rates arbitrarily, discouraging compliance, and causing considerable uncertainty among firms and households. One result of high tax rates has been widespread tax exemptions. Current proposals to roll back duty exemptions and lower tariff rates therefore deserve serious consideration. Broadening the coverage of trade taxes will facilitate elimination of the recently introduced 5 percentage point import declaration fee. Bringing noncash benefits into the tax net and applying customs duties to government imports in the l996 budget will also provide a needed broadening of the tax base. Nonetheless, it is difficult to see how the budget can absorb the cost of allowing ZCCM's temporary exemption from the VAT to lapse--the original intention when this measure was taken in mid-l995--until alternative revenue sources .can be developed.

A stronger system of expenditure control will do much to avoid fiscal gaps emerging in the future. The strengthened oversight of defense expenditures is very much to be welcomed and must be sustained. Expenditure control is being further enhanced by the commitments monitoring system, which also provides early warning of the possibility of new arrears developing. But the most important challenge facing the authorities in framing the l996 budget will be adhering to the policy of restraining growth in the civil service wage bill to the targeted rate of inflation. It will also be important that most privatization receipts be used to reduce government borrowing, pending their use in future years for nonrecurrent expenditures consistent with the fiscal program.

The observance of programmed external debt service schedules, despite balance of payments assistance being much less than programmed, is to be commended. However, through September l995 the authorities were not able to keep to their schedule for clearance of domestic arrears from l994, despite severe restraint of new expenditures. In the remainder of l995, it is important that Zambia access the donor assistance that is expected to become available as quickly as possible. This will facilitate full clearance of domestic arrears, with little or no use of bank credit for the year as a whole. If signs emerge of a possible fiscal gap in the fourth quarter of 1995, it is important that the authorities implement their package of contingency measures.

As for structural reform of the public sector, the momentum established under the privatization program in l995 should be maintained and every effort should be made to speedily divest the Government of its majority shareholding in ZCcM. Zambia's prospects for eventually attaining external viability will be improved by placing custody of its copper resources in private hands. Civil service reform should be pursued vigorously in 1996, although some ingenuity will be necessary to find ways of making severance packages affordable. As long as retrenchment costs remain as high as they are now, the number of civil servants can only be reduced by attrition. It will also be important that the Government adhere to its policy of noninterference in agricultural markets. The current drought, and the attendant jump in maize meal prices, will tax the Government's resolve to stay out of maize marketing, but will also provide it with an opportunity to demonstrate its commitment to the liberalization of the agricultural sector.

The authorities have in general pursued a cautious monetary policy in recent years, but in the first half of this year there was a rapid liquidity expansion because of the massive amounts of fiscal and quasi-fiscal support provided to Meridien Bank. The failure of African Commercial Bank was less costly, but it is nonetheless imperative that any future commercial banking problems be resolved quickly at minimum cost, and without compromising the authorities' ability to maintain the appropriate monetary policy stance. Adherence to the authorities' new policy of rapid response to incipient banking problems would do much to achieve these objectives. More fundamentally, the banking system must be shored up by the introduction of banking regulations consistent with international standards as quickly as possible, and strengthening the supervisory capacity of the Bank of Zambia should be a priority. The authorities' plans for other reforms in the financial sector, such as recapitalization of the Bank of Zambia, will also be important for the future conduct of monetary policy.

The authorities intend to continue steady implementation of monetary policy to meet the ceiling on the net domestic assets of the BoZ for endDecember, even if that should delay the expected decline in interest rates. Avoiding any resulting pressures on credit to the private sector would be facilitated by the Government continuing to refrain from using the full margin for domestic borrowing allowed for under the program. Moreover, the staff recommends that most of any buildup of international reserves beyond the program floor (itself a desirable objective) be sterilized to guard against a rekindling of inflationary pressures.

On the external side, Zambia needs to build up its international reserves to be able to better weather the shocks to which it is vulnerable, including fluctuations in donor flows. The authorities judiciously built up foreign exchange reserves through September, well ahead of program targets. In the last few months of l995, demand for foreign exchange will be boosted by maize imports and seasonal factors, but the end-December target for net international reserves should be attainable. It will be important for the
authorities to endeavor to achieve the medium-term targets for foreign reserves, and to be prepared to strengthen policies in the event of unforeseen shortfalls from these targets.

Zambia also needs to complete the process of regularizing its relations with external creditors. The outcome of current litigation by commercial creditors is uncertain, although Zambia is endeavoring to settle such claims on terms as close as possible to those accepted by other commercial creditors, which would also be in line with its undertakings to Paris Club creditors. Zambia will be seeking Paris Club flow rescheduling on Naples terms, and will in due course be seeking a Naples terms stock-of-debt operation. It must also move expeditiously to settle its arrears to other bilateral creditors on comparable terms. The welcome strengthening of Zambia's external debt service performance over the period of the RAP needs to be sustained, and will be assisted by the prudent policy regarding the incurrence of new external debt contained in the program.

Steadfast implementation of the program's policies in the period ahead will do much to bolster confidence in macroeconomic management, thereby reducing the uncertainty which deters potential domestic and foreign investors from commitment to the Zambian economy. National elections are to be held toward the end of 1996, and it will be essential. that the Government resist pressures to dilute its adjustment effort; given Zambia's new marketoriented macroeconomic structure, policy slippages will be quickly reflected in inflation, interest rates and exchange rates. Steadfast policy implementation will also do much to promote the mobilization of external support from official creditors and donors, from which Zambia will continue to need substantial external support on highly concessional terms for some years to come if it is to achieve external viability.
Proposed Decision
The following draft decision is proposed for adoption by the Executive Board:

1. Zambia has consulted with the Fund in accordance with paragraph 4 of the decision on accumulation of rights for Zambia (EBS/92/ll4, Supplement 2, 7/28/92), as amended, in order to reach understandings regarding the circumstances in which the accumulation of rights may be resumed.

2.  The Fund decides that, notwithstanding the nonobservance of the quantitative performance criterion relating to external payments arrears referred to in paragraph 4(a)(vi) of the decision on accumulation of rights for Zambia, as amended, and the structural performance criteria relating to government wage increases and Zambia Airways referred to in paragraph 4(a)(viii) of the same decision, Zambia may accumulate the remaining rights under that decision in an amount equivalent to SDR 183.l7 million.
