text: Dear Mr. Camdessus:

1.  The objectives of Zambia's new three-year program of economic and financial adjustment are set out in the policy framework (PFP) for the period l995-98, which was prepared in collaboration with the staff of the Fund and World Bank.

2.  The attached memorandum on the economic and financial policies of Zambia, based on the PFP referred to above, sets out the objectives and policies that the Government of Zambia intends to pursue in the three-year period covered by the PFP, as well as the objectives and policies for the first year's program thereunder. In support of these objectives and policies, the Government intends to request an arrangement under the structural adjustment facility (SAF) in an amount equivalent to SDR 181.75 million (50 percent of Zambia's new quota under the Ninth General Review) and parallel arrangements under the enhanced structural adjustment facility in an amount equivalent to SDR 70l.7 million (l93 percent of new quota). The first year's arrangements would be in an amount equivalent:to SDR 843.4 million (232 percent of new quota). The request would be made following the clearance of Zambia's arrears to the Fund and Zambia's consent to and payment for the increase in its quota under the Ninth General Review of Quotas.

3. In light of the corrective measures that have been implemented, which are described in the attached memorandum on economic and financial policies, and the objectives and policies that the Government intends to pursue during the three-year period covered by the PFP, the Government requests that the Fund approve the accumulation of the rights scheduled for November 15, 1994 and February 15, 1995 under Zambia's Rights Accumulation Programme in the amount of SDR 183.l7 million, notwithstanding the nonobservance of performance criteria on external arrears, Zambia Airways, and civil service wage increases.

4. The Government of Zambia will provide the Fund with such information as the Fund requests in connection with the progress made in implementing the economic and financial policies and achieving the objectives of the program described in the attached memorandum.

5. The Goverhment of Zambia believes that the policies and measures set forth in the attached memorandum are adequate to achieve the objectives of its program, but will take any further measures that may become

appropriate for this purpose. During the period of SAF/ESAF arrangements, the Government would consult with the Managing Director on the adoption of any measures that might be appropriate, at the initiative of the Government or whenever the Managing Director requested such a consultation. Moreover, after the period of the arrangements, and while Zambia had outstanding obligations to the Fund arising from loans under these arrangements, the Government would consult with the Fund from time to time, at the initiative of the Government or whenever the Managing Director requested consultation on Zambia's economic and financial policies.
6. The Fund would conduct with the Government of Zambia a midterm review under the first annual ESAF arrangement, to be completed not later than April 30, 1996.
Sincerely yours,
R.D.S. Penza Minister of Finance
Attachment

Mr. Michel Camdessus   
Managing Director   
International Monetary Fund Washington, DC 20431   
U.S.A.
1. This memorandum communicates the macroeconomic objectives and policies that the Government of Zambia intends to pursue during the three-year period beginning July 1, 1995.
I． Developments in the Second Half of 1994

2. The strict financial policies pursued in the second half of 1994 were consistent with further progress toward macroeconomic stabilization. Prices increased at an annualized rate of 22 percent, compared with 50 percent in both the first half of $1994$ and the last half of 1993. Interest rates on treasury bills declined steadily from May (when they exceeded 200 percent) to a mid-November level of l9 percent, thereby greatly reducing domestic debt service obligations. The exchange rate remained virtually unchanged at K 670 per U.S. dollar through November. Late in the year, however, there were some signs that the macroeconomic situation remained fragile. Inflation picked up, largely because of a sharp rise in maize meal prices, and the kwacha depreciated slightly, ending the year at K 683 per dollar. Interest rates rose to 24 percent at the end of the year.

3. While there were signs of recovery in some sectors of the economy, real GDP declined by 5.1 percent in $1994$ . This was attributable largely to a dry spell at the beginning of the year that hampered agricultural production and to management and technical difficulties in copper production. The current account of the balance of payments was stronger than expected in the second half of $1994$ .. reflecting low demand for imports and strong copper prices. Gross foreign reserves increased by $\mathtt{U S S11}$ million in the second half, bringing them to US $\$268$ million, equivalent to $2^{3}$ months of imports of nonmaize goods and nonfactor services. Monetary policy remained tight over the year. The monetary impact of the stronger-than-expected buildup of foreign reserves in the second half of the year was sterilized through a decline in net domestic assets of the Bank of Zambia by K 7 billion.

4.After some difficulties in the first half of $1994$ , the fiscal situation improved. Revenues in the second half of $1994$ exceeded the programmed level by 4.9 percent, while domestic noninterest cash expenditures were l.7 percent less than programmed, and there was net repayment of domestic financing. However, continued problems in controlling expenditure commitments detracted from the strong cash budgetary performance. During l994, line ministries committed K 4l billion in excess of fundings, accounting for 10 percent of total commitments.

5. In the area of parastatal reform, thirteen companies were privatized during l994 and two loss-making parastatals, Zambia Airways and United Bus Company of Zambia, went into liquidation. The privatization of ZCCM began with an offer to sell three mines.

6. Most performance criteria for September and December $1994$ were met, but in a few respects policies were not implemented in the second half of 1994 as we had intended. Fundings for personal emoluments (PEs) ran over programmed levels by some K 13 billion, largely because of excess fundings to the Ministry of Defence. (Such excess fundings were fungible for other uses, so that in effect PE expenditures were overstated.) The fiscal impact of this excess was more than offset by revenues exceeding programmed levels by K l9 billion, but it did point to problems in fiscal control, particularly with respect to defence, which we have taken steps to correct (paragraph 38). The impact of the June general wage increase itself was only K 1 billion over the programmed K 18 billion. This reflected an error in projecting the impact of the June wage increase, rather than any further increase, but it did involve a breach of the applicable structural performance criterion.

7.External arrears of $\mathtt{U S S25}$ million at the end of September 1994 indicated continuing shortcomings in debt service procedures. Since then we have further strengthened our procedures (paragraph 52). All but ${\tt U S}\$1.2$ million of programmed debt service payments falling due through end-December were met, and the ${\tt U S S1.2}$ million was settled early in 1995, thus permitting activation of the third tranche of the l992 Paris Club rescheduling agreement. However, early in l995, it was discovered that payments of $\boldsymbol{\mathrm{US}}\$18.8$ million to service a loan that was thought to have been forgiven had in fact been due prior to the end of l994. The creditor agreed that that amount, together with a further payment falling due in March 1995, could be repaid in l2 equal installments beginning in April l995; that payment schedule is being honored. Thus, although the end-December performance criterion on external arrears was breached, the problem was corrected promptly, and all payments have been made on schedule in 1995.

8. A decision by Cabinet on the future of Zambia Airways was taken on December 2, l994. This was later than we had expected, because we needed to examine some new proposals to restructure, rather than liquidate, the airline. Eventually we decided to proceed with liquidation. The budgetary limit on subventions to the airline was exceeded by K 0.9 billion during the airline's last weeks. The delay in deciding on the future of the airline and the excess over the budgetary limit constituted a breach of the applicable structural performance criterion.
9. All other performance criteria were met.
III. Developments in the First Half of 1995

10. At the end of l994, domestic maize stocks were being quickly depleted and it became clear that substantial maize imports would be needed during the first four months of l995. Maize prices rose steadily through February, reinforcing the effects of seasonal inflationary pressures stemming from holiday purchases and the rundown of government ministries' balances at the end of the fiscal year. Confidence was further shaken by the prospe. yet another severe crop shortfall in l995. These uncertainties reiniorc: the correction of the exchange rate and the renewed rise in interest. rates that were already underway.

ll. Moreovex. it became apparent: in January l995 that one of Zambia's largest private commrrcial banks, Meridien BIAO Zambia, was having a severs liquidity probien. i.::h mounted rapidly as the extent of its difficulties became common knowledge, culminating in a run on the bank in mid-February. Fearing contagion to the rest of the banking system, official support: was extended to Meridien through shifts in government deposits, secured loans, and current account overdrafts from the Bank of Zambia. We had hoped that the support would be only temporary, while the bank overcame its acute liquidity problems and the bank's owner arranged for additional capital injections. As time went on, however, it became clear that Meridien's problems went well beyond a lack of liquidity and that the capital that had been provided by the owner would not be sufficient. On March 27, the Bank of Zambia installed new management at Meridien, and on May l9 the Bank was put into receivership. At that time, total government and Bank of Zambia support to Meridien amounted to K 5l billion. In addition, the Bank of Zambia had $\mathtt{U S S25}$ million of its foreign exchange reserves lodged at Meridien's parent company, MIBL, which had been placed in liquidation by the Bahamian authorities.

12. Budgetary aspects of the fiscal situation also worsened markedly during the early part of the year as the rate of revenue collection declined and noninterest expenditures exceeded budgeted levels. A growing need for deficit financing at sharply higher interest rates was compounding the problem, and the prospect of a large fiscal deficit emerged.

13. On the expenditure side, defence expenditures and government wage payments became problematic. In the first four months of l995, defence fundings were K 2l billion, about K 9 billion more than would have been expected based on the annual budget of K 37 billion. Factors in the excess included K 2 billion to clear domestic arrears, K 1 billior each on wage increases, additional recruitment, and RDCs, and K 3 billion on capital expenditures. The capital budget provision for all of l995 was more than exceeded during the first four morths of the year, largely because the Ministry of Defence received advance fundings for construction and rehabilitation of military housing.

14. Negotiations on a wage increase for civil servants in l995 had begun in September 1994, eventually resulting in a stalemate whose resolution is still pending in the courts. The Government's position continues to be that no more than a 30 percent wage increase can be afforded. It began to pay that increase in March, retroactive to January. Teachers, whose unior. reached a separate agreement with the Government, received an additional 10 percent increase on July l. Doctors also received a pay award greater than 30 percent, and senior civil servants received an increase that made up for the fact. that they had received no increase in l994. Overall, the
budget allocation for PEs (excluding defence) and civil service reform now is projected to be exceeded by K l4 billion (or l0 percent), even without further wage increases.

15. The fiscal deficit and official support to Meridien resulted in large injections of liquidity over the first five months of l995, and net domestic assets of the Bank of Zambia rose over that period by l6 percent of the end$1994$ money supply. The Bank of Zambia was a net seller of foreign exchange so as to absorb liquidity and smooth the depreciation of the kwacha. Gross foreign exchange reserves declined by $\mathtt{U S S74}$ million, falling to ${\tt U S S194}$ million, and freely usable reserves were virtually exhausted. The availability of foreign exchange was constrained by the reduction in ZccM's surrender requirement to 30 percent in February as part of the ongoing liberalization, and by donor support being more than absorbed by external debt service requirements. Interest rates rose sharply, exceeding 50 percent by the end of May, and the kwacha depreciated beyond K 85O per U.S. dollar.

16. By May, it was clear that major policy adjustments would be needed to stem the ongoing injection of liquidity. The mid-May closure of Meridien was a first step toward regaining financial control. Control over other government expenditures was also enhanced at that time, but more farreaching fiscal action was needed to bring the budget back on track. Measures to increase revenues and strengthen expenditure control are at the core of the 1995/96 program.

17. Meanwhile, structural reforms continued. On February l, statutory reserve requirements were reduced from 27 percent to 3 percent, with the freed reserves being converted into government bonds with staggered maturities ranging up to 4 years and a market-based interest rate. ZIMCo, the parastatal holding company, was put into liquidation at the end of March 1995,and a unit was set up in the Ministry of Finance to assume its monitoring functions; the privatization of other parastatal enterprises accelerated.
IV. The Adjustment Strategy_for 1995/96-1997/98

18. The aim of the Government of Zambia's program for 1995/96-1997/98 is to move Zambia toward external viability and a higher growth path in a context of macroeconomic stability. The comprehensive reforms over the last three years that have freed the private sector from administrative controls have laid the foundations for such progress. Under the new program, Zambia is resuming progress on macroeconomic stabilization by redressing the slippages in financial control that occurred in the first part of l995, as well as carrying out further far-reaching structural reforms. The accompanying policy framework paper (PFP) lays out the details of our strategy for the next three years. 1/

19. With implementation of the policies outlined in this letter and in the PFP, Zambia should be able to achieve annual growth in real GDP of 6 percent in 1996-98. For l995, as a result of the drought, which has reduced the maize crop by almost a third, and lower copper output, real GDP is expected to show zero growth. If normal weather conditions return, agricultural potential should be more fully realized in l996-98, and nonagricultural growth will increase as the macroeconomic environment stabilizes. Manufacturing output can grow rapidly as idle capacity is brought back on line. Copper production is expected to recover slowly throughout the period as management and technical problems with mining and smelting operations are overcome. However, beginning the development of the Konkola Deep mine will help lay the foundation for future copper production. Sustained growth in electricity production can be achieved through utilization of excess capacity both to serve expanding domestic markets and to provide exports to the region. Sharp rises in maize prices in the remaining months of 1995 are likely to make it impossible for us to limit inflation in the year ending December 1995 to less than 35 percent, but with a fall in maize prices next year,we aim to limit inflation to 10 percent in l996, with further declines to 5 percent or less in l997 and 1998.

20. The improved macroeconomic environment, declining domestic debt service obligations, and continued progress in revenue collection will permit the Government to adopt budgets in l996-98 with larger allocations for capital expenditure, social sector spending, and reform of the civil service. On the revenue side, the Government intends to adopt a broad-based strategy conducive to the development of an equitable and efficient tax system. Within the parameters of its fiscal program, the Goverrment will attempt to provide assistance to those who are adversely affected by structural adjustment (training and support for retrenched workers, for example). As in the past, it will also endeavor to alleviate suffering caused by drought.

2l. Even after allowing for the losses on Meridien and the recapitalization of various other financial institutions (paragraph 48), our domestic debt remains relatively small--well below l0 percent of GDP--and will decline in future years. Our fiscal program is aimed at achieving an average domestic surplus of 1 percent of GDP, permitting reductions in domestic debt and freeing resources for private sector credit and the accumulation of foreign exchange reserves.

22. Ongoing revenue collections are projected to be maintained at 18-19 percent of GDP. Substantial improvements in tax administration should allow a reduction in distortions in the current tax system, including the lowering of some tax rates. The share of domestic government expenditure in GDP is projected to drop from 22 percent in $1994$ to l8 percent in 1998. This will be achieved largely through a reduction in interest payments and transfers related to agricultural marketing, as well as through limiting the growth of defence and general administrative expenditures. In formulating budgets over this period, our intention is that increases in the wage bill will be limited to no more than the rate of inflation, which will result in a gradual decline relative to GDP. Any wage award which implies an increase in the wage bill above the rate of inflation will be financed by revenue

measures rather than by compressing other expenditures. We will thus be able to make more adequate provision for social and capital expenditure, and will also be improving intra-sectoral allocations. The overall fiscal deficit on a commitments basis (including domestic and external components after grants) is targeted to improve from 6.2 percent of GDP in l994 to 2.5 percent in 1998.

23. Moving Zambia toward external viability will require us to develop our capacity to withstand the effects of external shocks such as fluctuations of weather and copper prices and the variability of donor flows. Over time, moreover, we need to reduce our dependence on copper receipts and donor assistance through strengthening the noncopper current account of the balance of payments. We intend to pursue these objectives through a strategy of increasing domestic savings and promoting exports. Besides encouraging economic growth, this strategy will result in an accumulation of external reserves, which will provide us with resources to smooth the effects of external shocks. A key element in the strategy will be the maintenance of a market-determined exchange rate in a context of rising external reserves and macroeconomic stability. Bank of Zambia operations in the exchange market will be aimed at achieving the desired accumulation of reserves, while smoothing sharp short-term fluctuations of the exchange rate; the BoZ will not attempt to use its reserves to defend an exchange rate which appears to be out of line with market fundamentals.

24. We recognize that the process of attaining external viability will take some years, but we intend to move strongly in that direction by building our external reserves to the equivalent of four months of imports of nonmaize goods and services by the year 2000. (Any windfall gains--for example from high copper prices--will go primarily to further increases in external reserves.) The strengthening of the balance of payments and the anticipated buoyancy of revenues resulting from high economic growth will mean that we will be able to meet from our own resources some of the large debt service obligations that will fall due beginning in 20oo. However, we expect to continue to need substantial balance of payments assistance for some time thereafter.

25. The Bank of Zambia will pursue a prudent monetary policy. As macroeconomic stability is consolidated and the economy recovers there will be a growth in demand for real balances. This should permit some real growth in the money supply, but we will provide liquidity to the market only when other indicators, such as inflation, the exchange rate, and interest rates indicate that it is prudent to do so.

26. There has been considerable progress in reforming the financial system in Zambia, but much remains to be done. Specific initiatives in this area are outlined in the PFP; the thrust of our efforts will be to modernize the legislation applicable to the Bank of Zambia, assure it of adequate operating income, minimize its operating cost, and increase its ability to execute its oversight responsibilities over the operations of commercial banks and other financial institutions. We will accelerate our program to strengthen banking supervision capacity within the Bank of Zambia to reduce the chance of problems similar to those of Meridien. We will continue efforts to place the responsibility for capital formation, investment, and credit decisions in the hands of the private sector. Specialized financial institutions currently owned by the Government will be privatized, or, if that is not possible, liquidated (paragraph 59 below). The Government will no longer provide budgetary funds t:o those institutions and will limit its support for them to providing state guarantees when required to do so by external lenders providing resources on highly concessional terms.

27. It is critically important that improvement in the allocation of resources continues and that the issue of improving public sector efficiency be addressed. Four areas are of particular importance if we are to move onto a path of long-term sustainable growth. First, the current pace of privatization--which has accelerated over the past year--must be maintained with completed sales reaching at least 90 companies by end-l996. Second, reform of the mining sector, particularly the privatization of ZCCM and private sector development of the copper ore body at Konkola Deep, both within the program period, will have far-reaching consequences for external viability and growth. In the interim, the Government will ensure that ZCCM management's actions are consistent with privatization plans. Third, sustained growth will require development of Zambia's agricultural potential, particularly through crop diversification. To this end, the Government will maintain its policy of nonintervention, with its role limited to research and agricultural extension services and maintenance of a strategic maize reserve. (A temporary exception is maize marketing, where the Government continues to provide some financing out of reflows from past loans; by 1997, the Government aims to leave such financing entirely to the private sector.) Fourth, a comprehensive restructuring of the civil service must be effected, resulting in a smaller, more efficient, and better paid civil service. Our goal is to reduce the size of the civil service substantially in the context of the Public Sector Reform Program: (PSRP).
V. The_1995/96 Program

28. The macroeconomic strategy of the 1995/96 program is to regain the: financial control established in the last half of $1994$ ， achieving our original 1995 budget objective (full clearance of domestic arrears without recourse to domestic financing), ard restoring our foreign exchange reserves to the end-l994 level by the end of l995; there will be further strengthening of fiscal and external positiors by the middle of l996. Structural reforms will focus particularly on the financial sector, the civil service, and privatization.
Fiscal policy

29. Measured on a commitments basis, the overall fiscal deficit for 1995, including both domestic and foreigr: components, and after grants, is projected to be 3.9'percent of GDP. As donor flows are projected to cover external debt service payments in l995, and much of capital expenditure is foreign financed, the Government will not have recourse to domestic

financing. This intention is reflected in a performance criterion on net claims on Government (Table 1). (The ceiling on net claims on Government for December 3l, 1995 includes a margin of K 10 billion over our budgetary target of zero financing. We intend to make use of this margin only if revenues fall short of projections.)

30. The system for monitoring government expenditure on a commitment basis, and for limiting domestic arrears, is now fully operational. In particular, monthly reports to the Ministry of Finance are required from all line ministries, including the Ministry of Defence. If these reports are not received on time, fundings are withheld pending their receipt. A task force has been set up to conduct spot audits of line ministries. The l994 arrears will be settled in l995. Avoidance of new arrears in l995 will be enforced by withholding recurrent departmental expenditure fundings to line ministries that run up arrears, and by disciplining controlling officers. Our aim to eliminate arrears accrued during $1994$ , and to avoid new arrears, is reflected in performance criteria (Table 1).

31. In June we resumed use of the "cash budget" to limit domestic expenditures. In addition, a Ministry of Finance committee meets weekly to consider line ministry requests for fundings in light of current revenues. As revenue flows become more predictable, we will adapt the system in such a way as to make monthly fundings also more predictable, thus offering ministries a better opportunity to synchronize their commitments and fundings.

32. Tax reforms in the l995 budget included measures to decrease double taxation of imported and other inputs and alleviate the excessive tax burden in some sectors. These included elimination of import duties on selected raw materials and buses and adjustment of income tax brackets. We also levied a 3 percent excise on electricity, for rural electrification, and increased the fuel levy, earmarked for road repair and construction.

33. The most important and far-reaching component of our tax reform efforts in 1995 has been the introduction of a value-added tax (VAT) on July l, 1995. The switch to a VAT did away with the cascading of the sales tax and broadened the tax base. The VAT is intended to make the tax system more stable and buoyant, enhance the competitiveness of Zambia's exporters, and bring the informal sector into the tax net.

34. In order to reduce the drain on revenues from statutory duty exemptions, the l995 budget speech announced the repeal of duty exemptions which were not specifically covered in statutes or international agreements. We have had to grant certain exemptions this year, but we are conscious of the danger that such ad hoc exemptions can exacerbate distortions and compromise our efforts to strengthen revenue collections, and will not grant any further exemptions by statutory instrument. (We intend to complete a rationalization of the custom duty structure by l998, with the first phase to be included in the l996 budget.) In August, we reduced the threshold for pre-shipment inspection to Us\$2,00o. This will further enhance our efforts to ensure duty compliance.

35. As it became evident that revenues would be insufficient to meet our fiscal targets for 1995 and 1996, we put in place additional measures. Some of the new revenue measures will be kept in place in future years if needed to meet our overall fiscal policy objectives. Given the magnitude of the fiscal problem, however, we have also had recourse to a temporary acrossthe-board increase in import duties of five percentage points, in the form of an import declaration fee. This increase still leaves our customs duties well below their levels at the start of our program of liberalization, and remains within the limits set by our World Trade Organization bindings, but we nonetheless intend to eliminate it no later than the end of 1996. At that time, we will replace it with other revenue measures if that is necessary to achieve our fiscal objectives. A list of all the measures taken is attached (Table 2). Together with some expenditure compression, these measures in total are projected to be sufficient to close the fiscal gap that emerged in the first half of l995 and yield an overall domestic cash surplus of 1 percent of GDP for the l995/96 program year. Enhancement of tax administration by the Zambia Revenue Authority will be critical to sustaining revenue levels and permitting further moderation in tax rates.

36. One factor in this year's budgetary problems was the wage increase for the civil service over the budgeted amount. No further wage increases will be granted by Government in l995. If any wage adjustment is made necessary by court action, it will be folded into the l996 wage increase, which will be determined in the context of the l996 budget and will be consistent with the policy described in paragraph 22.

37. One factor in the PE overrun in l994 was the payment of terminal benefits without budgetary provision. The budgetary PE allocation now provides for terminal benefits. We have also instituted monthly monitoring of PE expenditures and employment data, which will serve as an early warning system to avoid a repeat of the l994 overrun.

38. We have taken administrative measures to bring defence spending under control. First, an audit has been completed of all defence fundings in the first four months of the year and of outstanding commitments. Any additional multi-year commitments will require specific Ministry of Finance approval. Moreover, the Ministry of Defence is now included in the commitments monitoring system. Furthermore, after examining the defence payroll, the Ministry of Finance and the Ministry of Defence have agreed on the amount of the monthly allocations for PEs to the Ministry of Defence. No increase in the monthly allocations will be allowed without explicit authorization by the Ministry of Finance fundings committee. This will guard against fungibility of defence PE funding for other types of expenditure.

39. Zambia is participating in various regional UN peacekeeping efforts and receives financial support from the UN to defray the cost. In l995, the first K 6 billion of UN refunds will be divided equally between supplementary pay for soldiers serving abroad and capital improvements that will benefit military personnel in general. Half of any amount beyond K 6 billion will be paid to soldiers. The remairder will be used as general
revenue. In l996, we intend to revise this system so that all funds, except those required by UN regulations to go to soldiers, will supplement general revenue.
40.T The measures we have taken will ensure that funds will be available for key social sector expenditure and for necessary budgetary allocations for projects supported by donors.

41. The Government's program of civil service reform has begun. The restructuring plans for 3 ministries have been approved by Cabinet, and 6 others are currently being considered. The plans for the remaining 17 ministries (excluding the provincial administrations), are well underway, and we expect that by the end of the year all will have been completed for consideration by Cabinet.

42. It is essential that any redeployment decisions be consistent with the Government's macroeconomic targets and that the severance arrangements are affordable. Furthermore, the current cost of retrenching civil servants is so high that if any retrenchment is to occur, it will be necessary to make changes to pensions legislation or practices, which could include in-kind benefits to the extent possible and multi-year payments, possibly through a special government bond designed for this purpose. If it is possible to substantially reduce the cost of retrenchment via such changes, determined efforts will be made to mobilize resources to finance the retrenchment exercise. In addition to a provision in the l996 budget, donor assistance would be sought as an alternative source of funding for managing redundant personnel. These resources will cover severance packages, retraining programmes, and other social safety net provisions. An ODA funded consultancy is currently evaluating the various options of retrenchment to assist Government in identifying and adopting retrenchment packages which are cost. effective and affordable. However,we recognize that if it does not prove possible to substantially reduce the cost of retrenchment, the civil service can only be downsized through attrition.

43. In the meantime, there will be enhanced enforcement of the existing partial freeze on new hires. Only one new hire will be permitted for each three departures. This will lead to a decline in total government employment from its June l995 level of about l25,000 through attrition. A comprehensive manpower information system linking the payroll to the establishment register is being put in place. This system will help the Government monitor, on a regular basis, both those leaving and joining the civil service. We project that the size of the civil service will decline by 1,250 by December 1995; this projection is reflected in a structural benchmark.

44. As part of the on-going program of rationalizing civil service compensation, we are putting in place a new Social Security scheme, which will rationalize and standardize the modalities of paying retirees and retrenchees. Furthermore, we intend to ensure that pension payments to existing civil service retirees are brought up to date and that no new
arrears occur. We intend to develop a plan to address the issue of the cost of civil service reform by December l995 so that adequate provision can be made in the 1996 budget.

45. The l995 budget allows for expenditure of K 14 billion of receipts from privatization, and there is potential for considerably more if privatization plans go ahead as currently projected. The proceeds from privatization will eventually be spent on nonrecurrent. expenditures such as rural electrification and retrenchment of workers, but will be released only gradually for such purposes in light of the Government's overall fiscal objectives. In the immediate future, such proceeds will largely be used to reduce the Government's need for borrowing from the banking system.

46. The unwinding of the parastatal sector is likely to result in Government making some one-time payments to workers and other creditors of parastatals that have been closed or liquidated. For Zambia Airways, the Government paid terminal benefits totalling K 4.5 billion to its former employees in May and June l995. The Government, through ZIMCO, is expected eventually to receive sufficient funds from the liquidation of the airline to more than cover its outlays for terminal benefits. For UBz, terminal payments to workers will be made by the liquidator.

47. The Zambia National Oil Company (ZNoc) follows a market-oriented pricing policy aimed at full cost recovery, and adjusts prices as necessary to reflect world oil prices and changes in the exchange rate. At times in the past, however, necessary price adjustments have been delayed. In future, ZNoc will conduct a monthly analysis of the prices of its petroleum products, taking into account changes in the dollar price of crude oil and the exchange rate as of the last day of the month. The analysis will be transmitted to the Ministry of Finance by the l5th day of the subsequent month. When the analysis shows that a change in price of petroleum products amounting to more than 5 percent is warranted, zNoC will automatically effect that change by the end of the subsequent month, providing that the change remains warranted. Implementation of such price changes is subject to a structural performance criterion.

48. Reform of the financial sector will require outlays over the program period. The outcome of the Meridien Bank receivership is likely to have fiscal implications (paragraph 60). The Bank of Zambia requires recapitalization (paragraph 56). We will prepare an analysis of the financial situation of the Zambia National Commercial Bank to evaluate the need for its recapitalization in the context of the l996 budget.

49. So as to facilitate reducing domestic debt and freeing financial resources for use by the private sector, the l996 budget will aim at achieving a domestic surplus of at least l percent of GDP. To achieve this, we will follow a strategy of further reducing unproductive expenditures. The wage bill will be negotiated only after careful review of the overall budgetary envelope and will be consistent with the objective described in paragraph 22; given the potential threat to the program posed by excessive wage increases, this issue will be the subject of special attention at each review of the program. Concurrently, we will pursue some revenue measures to increase collections and rationalize the tax system, such as the reform of the customs duty structure referred to in paragraph 34 above. We will also examine the scope for limiting customs evasion by levying customs duties on government imports, and will adopt such a system in 1996. To increase income tax collection we will include noncash benefits in the tax base. We will study the impact of taxing agriculture and nontraditional exports on the same basis as other sectors, and will consider alternative ways of facilitating growth in these sectors. In view of the substantial revenue cost of integrating ZCCM into the VAT--now estimated at almost 2 percent of GDP per year--we will not terminate ZCCM's current VAT exemption until alternative measures are in place. The ZRA's tax collectio capability will continue to be enhanced through further computerization and improved communication facilities.
External policies

50. To enhance our ability to manage exchange market flows, on April 24 we increased the ZccM surrender requirement to 45 percent. Given our goal of building gross reserves to the equivalent of 4 months of imports by the end of the year 20oo, we intend to accumulate at least $\mathtt{U S}\$145$ million between July l,l995 and June 30, 1996. We will acquire at least $\mathtt{U S S85}$ million through normal market operations. Moreover, a contingency mechanism (Table 3) provides for additional sterilized reserve accumulation in the event of high levels of balance of payments assistance or high copper earnings. This is counted on to yield at least $\mathtt{U S S60}$ million in $1995/96$ and may generate substantially more. Timely receipt of donor assistance for essential maize imports will be important in helping us achieve our reserve objectives.

51. As noted in paragraph 53 below, some of our commercial creditors are litigating against us, and the eventual outcome may affect our ability to attain our reserve objectives. Moreover, some of our reserves have been held with institutions that are now in receivership, and full recovery is unlikely. The extent of our losses will be clearer at the time of the first review of the program. In developing policies for l996, we will take account of such losses, and undertake any strengthening of our macroeconomic policies needed to rebuild reserves quickly and maintain progress towards achievement of our medium-term objectives for gross reserves.

52. To strengthen the management of external debt, we have implemented a process for regularizing the payment of debt service obligations by formulating a detailed payments schedule. This schedule has also been communicated to Paris Club creditors for confirmation. The Bank of Zambia is authorized, on a quarterly basis, to make scheduled payments to creditors, and any discrepancies resulting from changes in interest rates or reconciliation with creditors will be settled during the next quarter. We have met all payments falling due in l995 so far, and our intention to incul no arrears is reflected in a performance criterion.

53. We intend to seek a Paris Club rescheduling on "Naples" terms covering much of the debt service falling due to Paris Club creditors from April 1, 1995 to the end of the period of the successor arrangement. We will also seek at least comparable debt relief from other bilateral creditors. Debt to commercial creditors had been largely eliminated through debt buy-back operations in $1994$ , and we stand ready to settle with our remaining commercial creditors on the same terms. Some commercial creditors are litigating against us because we have refused to offer terms more favorable than those accepted by our other creditors. In our dealings with such creditors, we will endeavor to adhere to the principle (agreed with the Paris Club and with those who accepted the buyback) of pari passu treatment. The stock of short-term debt of the public sector will decline by at least $\mathtt{U S S101}$ million from the end-June l995 level during the program period. There will be no new long-term external loan commitments except on concessional terms. There will be no external debt swaps other than those consistent with officially supported debt reduction operations. Other structural changes in the external sector are summarized in the PFP.
Monetary policy

54. Because of the setbacks in the first half of l995, we will need to be more cautious in our monetary policy than our experience from $1994$ might otherwise lead us to be. Real balances grew by some l percent per month last year, but in the first half of l995 they declined marginally. Thus, the monetary framework for 1995 assumes growth in broad money of about 30 percent, five percentage points less than the projected rate of inflation. For the first half of l996, broad money is assumed to grow by no more than the projected rate of inflation. Given our domestic budgetary goals and our external reserve objectives, private sector credit is projected to increase by $40$ percent during l995 and by 10 percent in the first half of 1996. Our monetary objectives are reflected in a performance criterion on net domestic assets of the Bank of Zambia (Table l). We also have developed targets for the level of reserve money, consistent with the projected growth in broad money, which will guide our actions in the absence of signs of an increase in the demand for money. Alternatively, if developments in inflation or the balance of payments suggest that demand for kwacha balances is weaker than projected, fiscal or monetary policy will be tightened as necessary.

55. Monetary policy will be effected largely through open market operations involving the purchase and sale of government securities. Because the Bank of Zambia's income is too small for it to absorb the cost of monetary policy, there will be an adjustment of up to K 5 billion in the ceiling on net claims on Government in l995 for the cost to Government of the Bank of Zambia effecting monetary policy through issuance of treasury bills. This is viewed as a short-term solution, pending recapitalization of the Bank (see next paragraph), and will not continue into l996 after the recapitalization takes place.

56. Although we have undertaken a substantial liberalization of our financial system over the past three years, there remains a need for further structural reform of the Bank of Zambia and the banking sector. For 1995, one objective is to modernize the laws governing the Bank of Zambia by revising the Bank of Zambia Act. We aim to implement the revised act before the end of the year. Most of the Bank's assets are noninterest-bearing government securities and the Bank has also incurred certain costs in carrying out government policy (e.g. support to Meridien Bank). To ensure a steady income flow to the Bank, the Government will need to recapitalize it. We will formulate a plan to this end by December l995 and incorporate the cost of recapitalization in the l996 budget. This undertaking is reflected in a structural benchmark.

57. Implementation of the Banking and Financial Services Act in December 1994 has set the stage for strengthened banking supervision. Regulations on capital adequacy and fixed assets, together with five other regulations, have already been put into effect. The Bank of Zambia intends to submit through the Ministry of Finance regulations on foreign exchange exposure in time for them to be gazetted by December 31, 1995; on large credit exposure and connected lending by February 29, l996; on provisioning by April 30, 1996; and on money laundering, together with any other necessary regulations, by August 3l, 1996. In addition, a regulation on the recovery of expenses incurred in financial system supervision will be drafted during 1995, and put in place at the time of approval of the BoZ budget for 1996. All such regulations will.be in line with internationally accepted (Basle) practices. Additionally, for loan loss provisioning, loan classifications based on time-specific criteria will trigger increasingly higher provisions. These undertakings are reflected in a structural performance criterion and structural benchmarks. Once the provisioning regulation has been completed, we will then undertake, over the next l2 months, to establish a system of prudential indicators to alert the Bank of Zambia to problems in the banking system, and we will establish objective criteria for determining bank solvency.

58. Much work has been done in l995 to develop the monetary survey as a tool for analyzing and monitoring monetary developments. However, softwarerelated difficulties in modernizing the accounting systems at the Bank of Zambia have resulted in continuing deficiencies in the Bank of Zambia balance sheet data underlying the survey. We expect that these difficulties will be overcome in the near future and that the monetary survey will be fully functional by the end of l995. Our aim is to use the monetary survey to monitor the financial program beginning in 1996.

59. As to reform of the parastatal financial sector, we intend to include Lima Bank, an agricultural lending institution, in the Zambia Privatization Agency's program for privatization or liquidation. Eximbank has also been included in the l995 tranche of companies being prepared for privatization. Early in l996, the Development Bank of Zambia will be restructured as an apex institution whose mandate would be to mobilize term resources from international concessionary sources for onlending to retail financial institutions.

60. During 1995 we have been preoccupied with banking problems, and need to make sure that such problems do not compromise our stabilization efforts in the future. Regarding Meridien Bank, in executing their responsibilities, the liquidators are considering all relevant options to maximize recoveries for the bank's depositors and creditors. We are also carrying out forensic accounting to provide a basis for attempting to recover funds lost through fraud or theft. At the time of the midterm review of the program, we will review our progress toward the objective of fully recovering official support.

61. The problems of Meridien Bank and, more recently, African Commercial Bank (which this month was placed in receivership) have shaken confidence and resulted in substantial financial outlays by the Government and the Bank of Zambia. To prevent such problems in the future we will, as noted above, strengthen the Bank of Zambia's supervisory and regulatory role. One specific step for the coming year will be to gradually increase the capital base of all banks, particularly smaller banks experiencing difficulties in meeting minimum capital requirements. In this regard, the Bank of Zambia has directed all banks to maintain a minimum capital base of K l.25 billion beginning June 30, 1996 and K 2 billion beginning December 31, 1996. Moreover, we will try to limit the cost of public support for troubled institutions in future by carrying out immediately an emergency on-site inspection of any bank that requires support by the Bank of Zambia in the form of uncollateralized overdrafts, or that has sustained difficulties in meeting requirements for holding liquid assets or statutory reserve bonds. Such an inspection will quickly provide the information needed for the Bank of Zambia to carry out its legal responsibilities for dealing with troubled banks.
Agricultural policy

62. Insufficient rains, particularly in the south of Zambia, and a decline in planting have resulted in a decline in agricultural production for the second year in a row. Although the drought conditions are not as acute as in 1992/93, emergency food aid of about 88 thousand metric tons of maize will be required. Donor support is also being requested to ensure the availability of sufficient foreign exchange for commercial imports of some 380 thousand metric tons of maize. This activity will be undertaken by the private sector, except for replenishment of Zambia's strategic maize reserve (which is provided for in the l995 budget).

63. The Government intends to maintain the maize marketing and input marketing revolving funds during l995/96. The former fund will be supplemented by the proceeds from the sale of the strategic reserve. The revolving funds will continue to be administered by banks on a commercial basis, in keeping with the Government's plan to leave such financing entirely to the private sector by l997.

64. We have reorganized the Zambia Privatization Agency (ZPA) to help it function more effectively. More than l0O companies have been approved for sale, out of a total number of about l60 parastatals. Modalities for the sale of these companies provide for local participation including management buyouts and sale of shares to the general public. By the end of September 1995, 26 companies had been privatized or liquidated. These companies accounted for more than one quarter of the total turnover of the parastatal sector, excluding ZCCM. The target of 25 companies privatized by year-end has therefore been exceeded.

65. The process of privatization of ZCCM has begun. A government mining privatization team has been formed to oversee the privatization of ZCCM on behalf of the Government. This team is responsible for the appointment and supervision of investment bankers, legal advisors, and other specialists as may be needed in the privatization process. Key advisors were appointed in September l995. The advisors will provide a detailed review of the rights and obligations of all the parties with a financial interest in ZCCM, and will prepare a privatization plan which will be submitted for Cabinet approval by March 31, 1996 and for ZcCM Board approval shortly thereafter. It is expected that execution of the plan will begin immediately after it is approved.

66. The Board of Directors and management of ZCCM have been informed of the decision to develop the Konkola Deep Mine as a majority private sector owned and privately managed project. The advisors on privatization will also develop a plan for the development of Konkola Deep.

67. We are aware that ZccM must be put on a sounder financial footing. Through our representatives on the Board of Directors, we will ensure that the company pursue strategies to reduce costs and increase production. Particular vigilance is required to contain the cost of employee compensation.
Statistics

68. We have made significant progress in improving statistical reporting capacities, particularly of monetary and fiscal data, but much remains to be done. Deficiencies remain in price statistics, the national accounts, and balance of payments statistics. By December l995, the Central Statistical Office (CSo) will complete the revision of the consumer price index based on the l993 household survey. The Government also intends to complete a new industrial survey in l996. With the household and industrial surveys complete, we will revise the national accounts, updating the underlying weights from l970 to 1993. New national accounts will be ready in 1996. In the context of updating the national income accounts, the CSo will improve the compilation and reporting of government financial statistics. We are revising merchandise trade statistics using complete customs data. Trade data are currently produced with a half-year time lag. The CSo will expedite the processing of customs data and reduce the reporting lag. The

Bank of Zambia and the CSO will coordinate efforts to improve the nontrade balance of payments statistics. In l996, the Ministry of Finance will develop a budget presentation that consolidates domestic and foreign components so as to strengthen the monitoring of fiscal developments.
Performance criteria

69. Performance criteria for December 3l, l995 are contained in Table 1 alorg with benchmarks for March 3l and June 30, l996. These include ceilings on net claims on Government, net domestic assets of the Bank of Zanbia, and the stocks of short-term and long-term nonconcessional debt. Also included are floors on net international reserves and prohibitions against accrual of external arrears and external debt swaps. There are structural performance criteria relating to petroleum product pricing, domestic arrears, and prudential banking regulations, and structural benchmarks on the size of the civil service, the formulation of a civil service reform plan, and the formulation of a'plan for recapitalizing the Bank of Zambia.

70. The Fund will conduct with Zambia a review of the program, to be completed no later than April 30, 1996. This review will include, inter alia, the financing of any real increase in the wage bill, progress towards privatization of ZCcM, and progress toward recovering official support for Meridien.

text: VII. Exchange Rate Arrangement:

All foreign exchange transactions take place at market-determined exchange rates. The official exchange rate of the Bank of Zambia is equal to the weighted average buying exchange rate quoted by commercial banks; a two percent spread is maintained to arrive at the Bank of Zambia selling rate. Transactions effected at the Bank of Zambia rate include purchases of $45$ percent of the foreign exchange earnings of the Zambia Consolidated Copper Mines (zCcM); the proceeds from external borrowing and donor assistance; and all government transactions, including debt service.

As a transition to a fully integrated foreign exchange interbank market, the Bank of Zambia conducts daily interbank dealing sessions, in which it entertains bids and offers from commercial banks to buy or sell foreign exchange, and decides on its purchases and sales in light of the price quoted and policy considerations. It is intended that this transitional foreign exchange market would be replaced by a single interbank market in the near future. On October 3l, 1995, the Bank of Zambia buying rate was $\texttt{K}932/\mathtt{U S}\$1$ ·

b. Zambia is on the standard 12-month Article IV consultation cycle; the last Article IV consultation was concluded by the Executive Board on September 23, 1994 (EBM/94/180). Directors welcomed the transformation of Zambia's economic system through the comprehensive liberalization that had taken place over the last three years. Directors stressed that Zambia had reached a critical stage in its development and regretted the lack of concrete progress in the structural reform of the public sector.
The following decision was adopted:

1. The Fund takes this decision relating to Zambia's exchange measures subject to Article VIII, Section 2(a) in light of the 1994 Article XIV consultation with Zambia conducted under Decision No. 5392-(77/63),adopted April 29, 1977, as amended (Surveillance over Exchange Rate Policies).

In view of Zambia's intention to eliminate this practice not later than end-l995, the Fund approves the retention by Zambia of that multiple currency practice until December 31, l995 or the conclusion of the next Article IV consultation with Zambia, whichever is earlier.

IX. Technical Assistance:

The Monetary and Exchange Affairs Department has furnished the Bank of Zambia with an Operations Advisor (August l990-August l992), a head of the Banking Supervision Department (January l99l to February 1993), Banking Supervision Advisor (from February l993), an advisor in the Research Department (from January 199l), an Advisor in the Accounting Department (from March l993), an Advisor on Foreign Exchange Operations (January-April l994), a Senior Operations Advisor (from September l994), and advisor on Monetary Policy (March $1994$ ， March 1996). The Fiscal Affairs Department provided an advisor on tax administration (August l992-July l993), and an advisor for macroeconomic and fiscal trainirg (August 1994-October 1994).

Technical assistance missions from the Fiscal Affairs Department visited Zambia in January l994, August l994, and February 1995 to advise on introduction of the value-added tax. Missions from the Statistics Department visited Lusaka in May l993 to review balance of payments data, and in October l994 to review the monetary survey. Technical assistance missions from the Monetary and Exchange Affairs Department visited Zambia in September l992 to review banking legislation, in March l993 to advise on government debt and monetary management, in March 1995 to review the implementation of the Capacity Building Project for the BoZ, and in October l995 to train the BoZ staff in financial programming. An STA staff member participated in the MAE March l995 mission, made recommendations for improving the Bank of Zambia balance sheet in order to compile monetary statistics according to the Fund's methodology, and also conducted training of Bank of Zambia officials in this methodology.
X. Resident Representative:
A Fund resident representative took up position in Lusaka in June l990. Since August 1995 the Fund resident representative has been Mr. Elwaleed Taha.

1. The World Bank Group has provided over 75 loans and credits to Zambia since l956, with total commitments amounting to ${\tt U S}\$2$ billion. These commitments have financed operations in several areas, including energy, transportation, communications, rural water supply, secondary and higher education, teacher training and technical education, commercial and smallholder agriculture (commercial crops, family farming, coffee production, dairying, integrated rural development), industrial forestry, and fisheries. Other loans and credits have supported the country's mining and manufacturing sectors and the urban sector. Two program loans were approved in the early l970s, and three adjustment credits were approved in the period 1985-86. Disbursements were suspended in May 1987 when Zambia abandoned the reform program and stopped paying debt service. Adjustment lending resumed after clearance of arrears in early l99l, and the latest adjustment operation was approved in July 1995. A free-standing technical assistance (TA) credit was approved in l978 to help the Government improve its planning and project preparation; a second TA credit was approved in 1986 to support improvements in economic management, a third was approved ir 1992 to assist in parastatal reform and privatization, and a fourth was approved in l994 to improve the management of the financial sector. The International Finance Corporation (IFC) has provided nine loans and six equity participations in support of private enterprise activities, including food production and processing, shoe manufacturing, packaging and plastic wrap, textiles, tourism, mining, and development finance.

Thus, the allocation for the 1992 policy adjustment credit was increased from $\mathtt{U S S100}$ million to $\tt U S\$200$ million, and some existing_operations were revised to provide funding for drought-related activities. In l993, an additional adjustment operation (PIRC II) was added to the program to offset the exceptional balance of payments need in that year, due in part to lower copper prices. In March $1994$ ， $\mathtt{U S S150}$ million Economic and Social Adjustment Credit (ESAC) was approved to supplement the Government's efforts in the promotion of the private sector and improve social services. The latest operation, the Economic Recovery and Investment Promotion Project (ERIP) approved in July 1995,involves $\mathtt{U S}\$135,6$ million, of which U $\cdot\mathsf{s s}28.2$ million has been disbursed. The Bank is also actively coordinating donor support for Zambia. The most recent Consultative Group meeting was held in Paris in December 1994.

4. Zambia's withdrawal rights under Bank loans and IDA credits were suspended on May 1, l987, because of arrears on obligations owed to the Bank. On March 5, 199l, the Bank approved, subject to the clearance of arrears, an Economic Recovery Credit (and supplemental IDA Reflows) in the amount of SDR l69 million ( $\mathtt{U S S237}$ million). On March 13,1991,Zambia cleared all its arrears to the World Bank and IDA, and disbursements resumed. Policy slippage in mid-l99l, particularly on maize pricing, led to a withdrawal of donor support, nonpayment of debt service, and a second suspension of disbursements in September. Following the elections in October, agreement was reached with the new government, the new arrears were cleared, and disbursements resumed in late January l992. Accordingly, the Bank's loans to Zambia are no longer ir nonaccrual status, and the Bank has ended its suspension of new lending and of disbursements of previously approved IBRD loans and IDA credits. text: 1 Structural reforms a. In the external sector:
The levels and dispersion of customs duties were reduced.
Quantitative controls on imports were removed.
The kwacha was allowed to float.
Exchange controls on external transactions (current and capital) were eliminated.
A "dealings" market was established as a vehicle for the Bank of Zambia's intervention in the foreign exchange market, pending the development of a full-fledged interbank market.
Zambia has become current on programmed external debt service payments, including the repayment of SDR 84 million in arrears to the Fund over the period of the RAP.
Relations with most external creditors have been regularized. Ad hoc debt swaps were terminated. Substantial debt reduction took place with respect to commercial creditors through a buy-back.

But little progress was made in negotiating settlements with non-Paris Club bilateral creditors. In one case, the debt has been assigned to a commercial debt trader, who is currently litigating against Zambia.
And problems in meeting structural and "governance" conditionality have led to fluctuations in donor balance of payments and, overall, much lower assistance than had been programmed.
b． In the financial sector:
Interest rates were freed from controls.
A market for government treasury bills was established.
Market-oriented techniques of implementing monetary policy were introduced.
Statutory reserve requirements (non-interest-bearing) were reduced from 27 percent to 3 percent.
Significant. improvements were made in the gathering and reporting of data critical to conducting monetary policy, though more remains to be done.
But delays in strengthening bank regulation and supervision to replace the oversight involved in direct controls have led to serious problems in some banks.
C In the real sector:
Price controls were eliminated, most notably in the agricultural sector.
The Government largely withdrew from direct involvement in agricultural marketing and the provision of agricultural inputs.
Parastatal companies were required to adjust their prices to cover costs.
Reform of land laws has taken place, although implementation is at an early stage.
A comprehensive privatization program was put in place, and implementation has recently accelerated.
But the process of privatizing ZccM has barely begun and the development of new mineral resources has been delayed.
d. In_the government sector:
The Government developed and increasingly operated under a "cash budget," which restricted funding of government operations to the availability of current revenues.
A commitments monitoring system was developed to avoid domestic arrears.
The collection of taxes was turned over to an independent revenue agency.
But the civil service remains large and underpaid, although reform has now begun.
Health services delivery was strengthened through the adoption of market-oriented policies.
But education reform has yet to begin.
2. Macroeconomic policy_problems and reform

The problems varied. In the first half of 1993, a major factor was rapid growth in Bank of Zambia (BoZ) credit to the oil distribution parastatal, which initially was masked by faulty accounting. Early in $1994$ ， there was a substantial revenue shortfall because of administrative breakdown prior to the establishment of the independent Zambia Revenue Authority (ZRA). In the early part of l995, budgetary problems stemmed in part from tax cuts (including reductions in reserve requirements, which had provided a large implicit tax levied through the banking system) that had been based on overly optimistic projections; expenditures, such as defense overruns, were also a problem; but the major destabilizing factor was the massive fiscal and quasi-fiscal support by the Government and the BoZ for the failing Meridien Bank. In each year, civil service wage increases exceeded budget and the fiscal problem was compounded by sharply rising interest rates; heavy external debt service requirements meant that when donor flows were interrupted in the first half of 1993, and again in l995, there were domestic fiscal pressures.

The immediate response to such problems has tended to focus on ad hoc revenue increases and expenditure cuts, but at the same time reforms have been introduced to deal with the fundamental sources of the problems. Their continuing effectiveness will of course require continuing close attention to program implementation.
Parastatal enterprises no longer can receive BoZ credit; they are required to operate on market principles, and those that have not been able to do so have been closed.

The establishment of the ZRA in l994 and the introduction of a value-added tax in July of this year have strengthened revenues. In the context of the l996 budget, the authorities plan to introduce a number of reforms to rationalize the tax structure, permitting revenueneutral reductions in some rates. 1/

1/ The l99l-93 customs reforms reduced the dispersion and average level of customs duties, with a minimum rate of 20 percent. At the same time, exchange system liberalization meant that many domestic producers lost preferential access to foreign exchange, while regional customs integration hurt domestic producers competing with those in neighboring countries who faced lower duties on imported materials. The result was widespread exemptions, systematically under the Investment Code and ad hoc for specific items or individual companies, as well as difficulties in customs enforcement.
The current program emphasizes a strengthening of bank regulation and supervision, as well as tighter control on official financial support for problem banks. 1/

The cost of civil service wage increases should become more predictable as inflation comes down. More fundamentally, the Government has shown increasing firmness in wage negotiations. 2/ Moreover, current policy emphasizes that real wage increases can only come through reductions in force, and a mechanism for gradual downsizing has been put in place.
Control mechanisms for defense expenditure have been strengthened.

Monetary targeting has been revised to emphasize that inflation control cannot be achieved at the expense of the external sector. (In the second half of l993, adherence to a tight reserve money target in the face of strong demand for money led to a sharp appreciation of the kwacha, the reversal of which at the end of the year added to instability in early 1994.)

Cash debt service requirements, which exceeded one third of exports and one half of fiscal revenues in l992-95, will drop substantially with the clearance of arrears to the Fund and a Naples terms Paris Club rescheduling.

1/ The remaining large banks have reasonably strong balance sheets and are either owned by large well-established foreign banks or, in the case of the largest bank, by the Government. Some of the many smaller banks may eventually be merged or closed, but any losses would likely be small in scale.
2/ This extended to ZCcM's salary increases this year, where the Government insisted that the increase could not exceed that granted to civil servants.
